advantages: Separating train, validation, and test gives each development phase a clear role — fit, select, and sign off — without conflating metrics.
A dedicated validation set lets teams iterate on hyperparameters freely while keeping the test set unbiased for final reporting.
Explicit splits make leakage easier to audit — any row in validation or test must never appear in fit() or preprocessing statistics.
Stratified and group-aware split options let practitioners match evaluation to deployment reality (class balance, time order, entity boundaries).
The three-way pattern is simple to explain to stakeholders: validation guides choices, test is the final exam.
disadvantages: Fixed hold-out validation and test slices reduce data available for training — painful on small datasets where every row matters.
A single validation set can be noisy — one unlucky split may favor the wrong model unless cross-validation supplements selection.
Choosing split ratios is problem-dependent; there is no universal 70/15/15 rule that works for all data sizes and tasks.
Improper split strategy (random shuffle on time series or user data) produces misleading validation and test scores that do not transfer to production.
Maintaining discipline on a locked test set is socially hard — repeated 'just one more peek' at test metrics erodes the estimate over a project.
